title: Ethnicity
examTip: Avoid vague phrases like 'ethnic minorities face discrimination' — be specific. Name the type (individual, institutional, cultural racism), cite a study (Macpherson, 1999; Nuffield College audit, 2019) and explain the mechanism.
introTips: Race and ethnicity are different things — and understanding why that distinction matters is your first step.
Ethnic inequalities show up in employment, housing, health, education AND criminal justice — learn at least one statistic for each.
The Macpherson Report (1999) on institutional racism is one of the most cited documents in GCSE sociology — know it.
intro: Why do people with identical CVs get fewer job interviews if their name sounds 'foreign'? Why are Black women four times more likely to die in childbirth than white women? These aren't coincidences or individual bad luck — they are patterns that repeat across millions of interactions, and sociology gives us the tools to understand why.

keyPoints.heading: Ethnicity, Race and Identity
keyPoints.content: Race is a socially constructed category based on perceived physical differences — historically used to justify colonialism, slavery and apartheid. Modern genetics shows that biological races do not exist: there is more genetic variation within perceived racial groups than between them. Ethnicity refers to a shared cultural identity — language, religion, history, customs and traditions. Sociologists prefer ethnicity because it captures how people actually identify, rather than imposing arbitrary biological categories. Ethnic identity is complex and fluid. Stuart Hall (1990) argued that Caribbean-British people hold a diaspora identity, navigating between two cultural frames — neither fully 'from' the Caribbean nor fully 'English.' Steven Vertovec (2007) coined super-diversity to describe cities like London where communities from hundreds of origins coexist — diversity within diversity, making simple categories inadequate.
writeBullets: Race — socially constructed category based on perceived physical differences; used to justify colonialism and slavery
Biological races don't exist — more genetic variation within groups than between them
Ethnicity — shared cultural identity: language, religion, history, traditions
Stuart Hall (1990) — diaspora identity: Caribbean-British people navigate between two cultural frames
Super-diversity (Vertovec, 2007) — cities like London have diversity within diversity
Ethnic identity is complex, multiple and situational — not fixed or singular

keyTerms.def: A socially constructed category based on perceived physical differences — used historically to justify exploitation
Shared cultural identity based on language, religion, history and traditions — more sociologically useful than race
Stuart Hall's concept — the identity of people displaced from their origin culture who navigate between two cultural worlds
Vertovec's (2007) term for cities where there is diversity within diverse communities — hundreds of different origins coexist
The idea that categories like 'race' are created by society rather than having a fixed biological reality

keyPoints.memTip: Race = physical (invented). Ethnicity = cultural (lived). Hall = between two worlds. Vertovec = diversity within diversity.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often use 'race' and 'ethnicity' interchangeably — insist on the distinction in exam answers.

keyPoints.heading: Ethnic Inequalities in the UK
keyPoints.content: Ethnic inequalities in the UK are documented across every major life domain. Employment: Pakistani and Bangladeshi men have employment rates of around 65%, compared to 80% for white British men (ONS). Pay: ethnic minority workers face a pay gap at all levels of qualification, even controlling for education. Poverty: 44% of Bangladeshi households live in poverty, compared to 18% overall (Joseph Rowntree Foundation). Housing: South Asian and Black households experience higher rates of overcrowding; audit studies show that ethnic-sounding names receive fewer responses in the private rental market. Health: Black women are four times more likely to die in childbirth than white women (MBRRACE-UK, 2021). Education: varies significantly by ethnic group — detailed in Topic S2. Criminal justice: Black people are nine times more likely to be stopped and searched than white people (Home Office, 2020). These are not isolated individual incidents — they are structural patterns produced by embedded inequalities across institutions.
writeBullets: Employment — Pakistani/Bangladeshi men: ~65% employment rate vs ~80% white British (ONS)
Poverty — 44% of Bangladeshi households in poverty vs 18% overall (Joseph Rowntree Foundation)
Housing — overcrowding higher in South Asian and Black communities; discrimination in rental market
Health — Black women 4× more likely to die in childbirth than white women (MBRRACE-UK, 2021)
Criminal justice — Black people 9× more likely to be stopped and searched (Home Office, 2020)
These are structural patterns across institutions — not individual incidents

keyTerms.def: The difference in average earnings between ethnic minority workers and white workers — persists even at similar qualification levels
UK body that monitors maternal mortality — found Black women 4× more likely to die in childbirth than white women (2021)
Inequality embedded in the organisations, rules and practices of society — not just individual prejudice

keyPoints.memTip: For health: Black maternal mortality is 4×. For CJS: stop and search is 9×. For poverty: Bangladeshi households at 44%.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Encourage students to remember 2–3 sharp statistics rather than vaguely saying 'ethnic minorities do worse' — precision scores marks.

keyPoints.heading: Explanations for Ethnic Inequalities
keyPoints.content: Sociologists offer several explanations for ethnic inequalities. Cultural explanations suggest that some groups' values and behaviours promote achievement while others face cultural barriers. CRITICISM: this risks victim-blaming, treating culture as a fixed deficiency rather than a response to structural disadvantage. Structural racism — the idea that institutions systematically disadvantage ethnic minorities through their policies, procedures and unconscious bias — was made prominent by the Macpherson Report (1999), written after the Metropolitan Police's flawed investigation into the murder of Stephen Lawrence. Macpherson defined institutional racism as 'the collective failure of an organisation to provide appropriate professional service to people because of their colour, culture or ethnic origin — through unwitting prejudice, ignorance, thoughtlessness and racist stereotyping.' Direct discrimination is documented by the Nuffield College audit study (2019): identical CVs with ethnic-sounding names received 60% fewer interview callbacks than those with white-sounding names. Post-colonial theory (Paul Gilroy — 'There Ain't No Black in the Union Jack,' 1987) situates current inequalities in the history of colonialism — economic exploitation that created global hierarchies still felt today.
writeBullets: Cultural explanations — values/behaviour differences between groups; CRITICISM: victim-blaming, ignores structural causes
Institutional racism — Macpherson (1999): collective organisational failure to serve people equally, through unwitting prejudice
Stephen Lawrence murder — exposed institutional racism in the Metropolitan Police (Macpherson Report, 1999)
Nuffield College audit study (2019) — ethnic-sounding CVs got 60% fewer interview callbacks than identical white-sounding CVs
Post-colonial theory — Gilroy (1987): current inequalities rooted in history of colonial exploitation
Structural racism produces patterns across institutions — not reducible to individual prejudice

keyTerms.def: Macpherson's (1999) definition: the collective failure of organisations to serve people equally due to unwitting prejudice, ignorance and racist stereotyping
1999 report into the Metropolitan Police's investigation of Stephen Lawrence's murder — identified institutional racism in the force
A research method sending identical CVs or applications with different names to measure discrimination
Sociologist who wrote 'There Ain't No Black in the Union Jack' (1987) — argues Black Britishness is marginalised in national identity
The view that current racial inequalities are rooted in the history of colonialism and its lasting economic and cultural effects

keyPoints.memTip: Macpherson = institutional racism named. Nuffield 2019 = 60% fewer callbacks. Gilroy = colonial history still shapes today.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The Macpherson definition is worth memorising word-for-word — it appears directly in mark schemes.

keyPoints.heading: Racism and Responses
keyPoints.content: Racism takes several forms. Individual racism is personal prejudice and discriminatory behaviour — conscious acts of hostility or unfairness based on ethnicity. Institutional racism (Macpherson, 1999) is embedded in organisational practices and culture — it can operate even without individually racist people. Cultural racism substitutes 'culture' for 'race' to justify exclusion: arguing that certain groups are culturally incompatible, rather than using explicitly racial language. Islamophobia increased dramatically after 9/11 — there were over 47,000 race and religion hate crimes reported in England and Wales in 2022 (Home Office). Societal responses include: the Equality Act 2010 (which consolidated and extended anti-discrimination law), positive action programmes that allow employers to favour under-represented groups in tie-break situations, diversity monitoring, and community cohesion policies. A central debate in contemporary sociology asks whether Britain is genuinely more tolerant than in previous decades — hate crime reporting has increased, but it is unclear whether this reflects more racism or more willingness to report. Structural racism persists regardless of legal progress, as demonstrated by continuing inequalities in employment, health and criminal justice.
writeBullets: Individual racism — personal prejudice and discriminatory behaviour
Institutional racism — embedded in organisations, operates even without consciously racist individuals (Macpherson, 1999)
Cultural racism — 'culture' used instead of 'race' to justify exclusion
Islamophobia — 47,000+ race/religion hate crimes reported in 2022 (Home Office)
Equality Act 2010 — consolidated anti-discrimination law; positive action allowed (not positive discrimination)
Debate: is Britain more tolerant, or does structural racism persist behind legal progress?

keyTerms.def: Conscious personal prejudice or discrimination based on ethnicity or perceived race
Using cultural difference rather than explicit racial language to justify exclusion or discrimination
Prejudice and discrimination against Muslims — increased significantly after 9/11
UK legislation consolidating and extending legal protection against discrimination on grounds of race, gender, disability and other characteristics
Measures that allow employers to favour underrepresented groups in a tie-break — legal under the Equality Act 2010 (not the same as positive discrimination)

keyPoints.memTip: Three types of racism: Individual (person), Institutional (organisation), Cultural (coded language). All three need tackling.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often confuse positive action (legal) with positive discrimination (illegal in the UK) — clarify this distinction.

tables.title: Ethnic Inequalities: Evidence Across Key Domains
rows: Pakistani/Bangladeshi men: ~65% employment rate vs ~80% white British
Ethnic minority pay gap persists at all qualification levels
44% Bangladeshi households in poverty vs 18% overall (JRF)
Black women 4× more likely to die in childbirth (MBRRACE-UK, 2021)
Black people 9× more likely to be stopped and searched (Home Office, 2020)
Overcrowding and rental discrimination; ethnic-sounding names get fewer callbacks

0.question: What is the difference between 'race' and 'ethnicity' in sociology?
options: Race is a socially constructed category based on physical differences; ethnicity refers to shared cultural identity
Race is about culture and tradition; ethnicity is about biological differences between groups
Race and ethnicity are interchangeable terms meaning the same thing
Race is determined by birthplace; ethnicity is determined by the colour of a person's skin
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Sociologists prefer 'ethnicity' because 'race' is a social construction with no biological basis — there is more genetic variation within so-called racial groups than between them.
0.wrongFeedback: These terms are often confused. 'Race' is a socially constructed category (based on perceived physical differences) with no biological reality. 'Ethnicity' is the more useful term — it refers to shared cultural identity: language, religion, history and traditions.

2.question: What did the Nuffield College audit study (2019) find?
options: CVs with ethnic-sounding names received 60% fewer interview callbacks than identical CVs with white-sounding names
Ethnic minority candidates were offered higher salaries than white candidates with the same qualifications
There was no significant difference in callback rates between ethnic-sounding and white-sounding names
Ethnic minority applicants were more likely to be shortlisted when applying for public sector jobs
2.correctFeedback: Correct. The Nuffield College (2019) audit study is a key piece of evidence for direct discrimination in the labour market — identical CVs, significantly different outcomes.
2.wrongFeedback: The Nuffield College audit study (2019) sent identical CVs with different names and found ethnic-sounding names received 60% fewer callbacks — this is direct evidence of employment discrimination.

commonMistakes: Using 'race' and 'ethnicity' interchangeably — they are different: race is a social construction based on physical appearance; ethnicity is shared cultural identity.
Giving only cultural explanations for ethnic inequality — this risks victim-blaming; always include structural racism and institutional racism as alternative explanations.
Forgetting that institutional racism can occur even without individual racist intent — the Macpherson definition is explicit that 'unwitting prejudice' counts.
Treating 'ethnic minorities' as one uniform group — different ethnic groups have very different experiences of inequality (e.g., Chinese British students outperform white British students at GCSE).
Confusing positive action (legal — favouring underrepresented groups in a tie-break) with positive discrimination (illegal in the UK — selecting someone solely because of their ethnicity).
examTips: Name specific studies and statistics — the Macpherson Report (1999), the Nuffield College audit (2019) and the MBRRACE-UK maternal mortality data all appear in mark schemes.
Use the three-level racism framework (individual, institutional, cultural) to show that you understand racism operates at multiple levels.
When evaluating cultural explanations, always counter with structural racism — show you know both sides.
Intersectionality is a strong evaluative tool: a Black working-class woman faces compounded disadvantages (Crenshaw, 1989) — use it to earn top-band marks.
revisionCardBullets: Race — socially constructed; no biological basis; used to justify colonialism
Ethnicity — shared cultural identity: language, religion, history, traditions
Stuart Hall (1990) — diaspora identity: navigating between two cultural worlds
Super-diversity (Vertovec, 2007) — diversity within diversity in cities like London
Employment gap — Pakistani/Bangladeshi men ~65% employed vs white British ~80%
Poverty — 44% of Bangladeshi households in poverty vs 18% overall (JRF)
Black women 4× more likely to die in childbirth (MBRRACE-UK, 2021)
Black people 9× more likely to be stopped and searched (Home Office, 2020)
Nuffield audit (2019) — ethnic-sounding CVs got 60% fewer callbacks
Macpherson (1999) — institutional racism: collective organisational failure
Gilroy (1987) — 'There Ain't No Black in the Union Jack': colonial history shapes today
Equality Act 2010 — consolidated anti-discrimination law; positive action (not discrimination) allowed
